text: This is a case of a robotic-assisted bilateral adrenalectomy for pheochromocytoma. The procedure was performed at Sinai Grace Hospital, a part of the Detroit Medical Center Department of Surgery. A forty-two-year-old female with a past medical history of SLE, von Hippel-Lindau disease, bilateral pheochromocytomas, and type II diabetes, as well as hypertension, which required three separate antihypertensive medications at max doses for blood pressure control, presented to the emergency department with intermittent headaches, palpitations, generalized weakness, and hyperepigmentation for the past two months period. due to her strong family history which included both of her sons who were diagnosed with and treated for the disease at ages six and thirteen. The patient had initially refused surgery however because her symptoms had worsened she presented for definitive care.

Laboratory analysis was performed as well as imaging studies including a CT, MRI and PET scans were performed and a final diagnosis of bilateral pheochromocytomas was made. Preoperatively, the patient was first alpha-blocked, then beta-blocked, and also given adequate preoperative hydration.

The robotic XI platform is utilized with the patient in the left lateral decubitus position. The surgeon begins dissection using Maryland monopolar scissors, taking down the splenic flexure of the colon and colosplenic ligaments. The spleen and tail of the pancreas are mobilized, exposing the adrenal mass. The mass is carefully removed from the tail of the pancreas and spleen. A vein-first approach is used, identifying the renal vein and adrenal vein, which is dissected away from surrounding tissues. Hemoclips are applied to isolate and divide the adrenal vein.

The surgeon isolates a second adrenal vein, applying hemoclips before dividing it. The spleen is visible on the left side of the screen, while the kidney is located at the bottom of the dissection plane. The adrenal mass is carefully dissected free from surrounding tissues and placed into an endocatch bag, which is tagged to the abdominal wall for later removal. The patient is repositioned to the opposite side, with an additional port added in the right lower quadrant.

The surgeon begins the procedure by taking down the hepatic flexure and mobilizing the duodenum. The adrenal mass is visible, and a liver retractor is used to elevate the liver for better exposure. Electrocautery is employed to carefully dissect the mass from surrounding tissues. Due to the tumor's position over the IVC, the vein-first approach is not feasible. Girota's fascia is incised, and the adrenal gland is separated from the superior pole of the kidney. Dissection continues posteriorly until the diaphragm and psoas muscle are visible. Small arterial branches are clipped with hemoclips, and the tumor side is cauterized and cut with monopolar scissors. The adrenal vein is identified, clipped, and divided, ensuring hemostasis. The adrenal gland is freed from its attachments, removed, and placed in an endo-catch bag.

Total console time was one hour and forty one minutes and the patient was discharged on post-operative day number three because endocrinology wanted to keep the patient to make sure that adequate steroid management was achieved before the patient was sent home. It should be noted that the patient did not require any further blood pressure medications.
Final pathologic diagnosis was consistent with pheochromocytomas of the bilateral adrenal glands with the sizes of the tumors noted on this slide. The final pathologic stage was noted to be PT-NX.

This is a retrospective review out of the Cleveland Clinic where one surgeon compared his robotic adrenalectomy versus his laparoscopic adrenalectomy data. The indications for surgery and contraindications are pretty standard and noted. in this slide here. And the standard workup was performed in all of these patients. However, there were thirty patients in the robotic arm compared to forty-six patients in the laparoscopic arm. No difference in demographics were noted. There was a slight higher ASA score in the robotic arm compared to a slightly larger tumor size in the laparoscopic arm. However, no difference was noted in terms of operative times, estimated blood loss, length of stay, use of morphine equivalents, intraoperative complications, postoperative complications, including transfusions, or any other significant markers.
Therefore, they concluded that the robotic adrenalectomy is a safe and effective approach to adrenal surgery. Thank you very much.
